curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Use and justify the Sine Rule to find missing sides or angles in any triangle, and connect the rule to proof ideas using perpendicular heights.

verse_ality.driving_question: How can we prove the Sine Rule works for any triangle — not just right-angled ones?

knowing_world.focus: Learn and apply the Sine Rule relationship between each side and the sine of its opposite angle, then explore how a perpendicular height can be used to derive the rule.
key_concepts: Opposite side and opposite angle matching
Finding a missing side using a/sin(A) = b/sin(B)
Finding a missing angle using a/sin(A) = b/sin(B)
Using perpendicular height to derive/prove the relationship
Accuracy, checking, and reasonableness in triangle solutions
tools: Video Part 2: Worked solutions + deriving the Sine Rule (canva_embed): https://www.canva.com/design/DAGvY5Lm9ME/aKWZsBhMor7XHzAU_WBKoQ/watch?embed
activities: Identify matching pairs (side a opposite angle A, etc.) and set up the correct sine rule proportion.
Solve at least one missing side problem using the Sine Rule.
Solve at least one missing angle problem using the Sine Rule.
Explain (in words) why dropping a perpendicular height helps derive the Sine Rule.

human_story.focus: See how the Sine Rule supports triangulation in navigation, surveying, astronomy, architecture, and 3D modelling, where right angles are not available.
justice_prompts: How would life change if we didn’t have tools to calculate inaccessible distances or angles?
What kinds of people or professions rely on trigonometry to connect unknowns?

expressive_self.focus: Internalise the idea that sides ‘look across’ to opposite angles and communicate the rule through metaphor, comics, and explanation.
activities: Sketch a triangle and decorate each side/angle with metaphors (e.g., ‘Side a looks across to Angle A’).
Create a short comic strip where triangle characters discover the Sine Rule.
Reflect: What helped you most in understanding the Sine Rule derivation?
Explain the Sine Rule to someone who thinks trig is only for right-angled triangles.

designed_world.focus: Use an interactive triangle sketch to generate non-right-angled triangles, hide/reveal values, challenge peers, and use perpendicular constructions to derive the Sine Rule.
design_challenges: Drag triangle points to generate different non-right-angled triangles.
Challenge a peer: hide two side lengths and ask them to find them using the Sine Rule.
Reveal heights (perpendicular constructions) to derive and prove the Sine Rule algebraically.
Use buttons to hide or reveal angles and side lengths.
Ethical design: adapt geometry tools for students with visual or fine-motor challenges.
tools: Interactive sketch: Sine Rule Triangle Sketch (canvas_file_embed): https://canvas.instructure.com/courses/12822206/files/314020098/download

regulation_supports: Make a triangle with your arms and head and hold the pose.
Breathe in for 4 seconds as you ‘fill’ one side of your triangle.
Exhale for 6 seconds while tracing another side in the air.
Let your shoulders drop and reset after every ‘proof’.
wellbeing_prompts: How do physical breaks help your mind process triangle logic?

pathways.name: Final Task: Triangle Translator (SDG 4)
pathways.focus: Create and solve two unique triangles in a web sketchpad—one missing side problem and one missing angle problem—then clearly explain and verify solutions using the Sine Rule.
products: Two unique triangles created in the interactive sketch tool (screenshots).
In one triangle: calculated missing side with clear working.
In the other triangle: calculated missing angle with clear working.
Explanation (writing, diagrams, or short video) including how you verified answers using the Sine Rule.

required: Basic trig ratios in right-angled triangles (sin, cos, tan) and what sine represents
Using a calculator for sine values (degree mode where appropriate)
Triangle angle facts (sum of angles in a triangle)
Understanding of opposite sides and angles in triangles

builds_toward: Area of a triangle using 1/2ab sin(C)
Solving non-right-angled triangle problems in 2D and 3D contexts
Further trigonometry problem solving (bearings, navigation, surveying)
